label: Formula 30.9b
section: SELECTION ON MULTIVARIATE PHENOTYPES: DIFFERENTIALS AND GRADIENTS
subsection: Changes in the Covariance Matrix: The Quadratic Selection Differential Matrix, C
nearby_text: When $ z_{i} $ and $ z_{j} $ are bivariate-normal, then (Kendall and Stuart 1983), $$ \sigma^{2}[(z_{i}-\mu_{i})(z_{j}-\mu_{j})]=P_{i j}^{2}+P_{i i}P_{j j}=P_{i j}^{2}(1+\rho_{i j}^{-2}) $$ (30.9b) where $ \rho_{ij} $ is the phenotypic covariance between $ z_{i} $ and $ z_{j} $. Hence, for Gaussian-distributed phenotypes, $$ \left|\frac{C_{ij}}{P_{ij}}\right|\leq\sqrt{I}\sqrt{1+\rho_{ij}^{-2}}

        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}(\\mathbf{z}-\\boldsymbol{\\mu}^{*})(\\mathbf{z}-\\boldsymbol{\\mu}^{*})^{T}&=(\\mathbf{z}-\\boldsymbol{\\mu}-\\mathbf{S})(\\mathbf{z}-\\boldsymbol{\\mu}-\\mathbf{S})^{T}\\\\&=(\\mathbf{z}-\\boldsymbol{\\mu}-\\mathbf{S})([\\mathbf{z}-\\boldsymbol{\\mu}]^{T}-\\mathbf{S}^{T})\\\\&=(\\mathbf{z}-\\boldsymbol{\\mu})(\\mathbf{z}-\\boldsymbol{\\mu})^{T}-(\\mathbf{z}-\\boldsymbol{\\mu})\\mathbf{S}^{T}-\\mathbf{S}\\left(\\mathbf{z}-\\boldsymbol{\\mu}\\right)^{T}+\\mathbf{S}\\mathbf{S}^{T}\\end{aligned}",

        "section": "SELECTION ON MULTIVARIATE PHENOTYPES: DIFFERENTIALS AND GRADIENTS",

label: Formula 30.12
section: SELECTION ON MULTIVARIATE PHENOTYPES: DIFFERENTIALS AND GRADIENTS
subsection: The Quadratic Selection Gradient Matrix, $ \gamma $
nearby_text: Using multiple regression theory, Lande and Arnold (1983) showed that when $ z \sim MVN $, the matrix, $ \gamma $, of quadratic partial regression coefficients is given by $$ \gamma=\mathbf{P}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\sigma}[w,(\mathbf{z}-\boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{z}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^{T}]\mathbf{P}^{-1}=\mathbf{P}^{-1}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{P}^{-1} $$ (30.12)

label: Formula 30.16
section: SELECTION ON MULTIVARIATE PHENOTYPES: DIFFERENTIALS AND GRADIENTS
subsection: Quadratic Gradients, Fitness Surface Geometry, and Selection Response
nearby_text: ypic selection alters the additive-genetic covariance matrix. As we show in Volume 3, the additive-genetic covariance matrix, $ G^{*} $, following selection (but before reproduction) is calculated by $$ \mathbf{G}^{*}=\mathbf{G}\left(\gamma-\beta\beta^{T}\right)\mathbf{G}+\mathbf{G} $$ (30.16)

label: Formula 30.18a
section: SELECTION ON MULTIVARIATE PHENOTYPES: DIFFERENTIALS AND GRADIENTS
subsection: Quadratic Gradients, Fitness Surface Geometry, and Selection Response
nearby_text: What about the effect of correlational selection ( $ \gamma_{ij} \neq 0 $? Again, assuming all correlations (genetic and phenotypic) are (initially) zero, Equation 30.16 yields $$ C_{i j}=\sigma(z_{i}^{*},z_{j}^{*})-\sigma(z_{i},z_{j})+S_{i}S_{j}=2\gamma_{i j}P_{i i}P_{j j} $$ (30.18a) and the within-generation change in the phenotypic covariance becomes $$ \delta\left[\sigma(z_{i},z_{j})\right]=2\gamma_{i j}P_{i i}P_{j j}-S_{i}S_{j} $$ (30.18b)

  "nearby_text": "What about the effect of correlational selection ( $ \\gamma_{ij} \\neq 0 $? Again, assuming all correlations (genetic and phenotypic) are (initially) zero, Equation 30.16 yields $$ C_{i j}=\\sigma(z_{i}^{*},z_{j}^{*})-\\sigma(z_{i},z_{j})+S_{i}S_{j}=2\\gamma_{i j}P_{i i}P_{j j} $$ (30.18a) and the within-generation change in the phenotypic covariance becomes $$ \\delta\\left[\\sigma(z_{i},z_{j})\\right]=2\\gamma_{i j}P_{i i}P_{j j}-S_{i}S_{j} $$ (30.18b)"
}

section: SELECTION ON MULTIVARIATE PHENOTYPES: DIFFERENTIALS AND GRADIENTS

nearby_text: they are selected in opposite directions, this increases the correlation. Assuming no (initial) genetic correlations, Equation 30.16 gives the (within-generation) change in the genetic covariance as $$ \delta\left[\sigma(A_{i},A_{j})\right]=\left(2\gamma_{i j}-\beta_{i}\beta_{j}\right)\sigma^{2}(A_{i})\sigma^{2}(A_{j}) $$ (30.18c)

label: Formula 30.19
section: MULTIDIMENSIONAL QUADRATIC FITNESS REGRESSIONS
subsection: Geometric Aspects
nearby_text: variate quadratic fitness regressions have a rather rich geometric structure. By adjusting the character values to give them a mean of zero, the general quadratic fitness regression can be written as $$ w(\mathbf{z})=a+\sum_{i=1}^{n}b_{1}z_{i}+\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\gamma_{ij}z_{i}z_{j}=a+\mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{z}+\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{z}^{T}\gamma\mathbf{z} $$ (30.19)

  "nearby_text": "variate quadratic fitness regressions have a rather rich geometric structure. By adjusting the character values to give them a mean of zero, the general quadratic fitness regression can be written as $$ w(\\mathbf{z})=a+\\sum_{i=1}^{n}b_{1}z_{i}+\\frac{1}{2}\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\sum_{j=1}^{n}\\gamma_{ij}z_{i}z_{j}=a+\\mathbf{b}^{T}\\mathbf{z}+\\frac{1}{2}\\mathbf{z}^{T}\\gamma\\mathbf{z} $$ (30.19)"
}

label: Formula 30.20a
section: MULTIDIMENSIONAL QUADRATIC FITNESS REGRESSIONS
subsection: Geometric Aspects
nearby_text: We start our exploration of this geometry by considering the gradient of this best-fitting quadratic fitness surface. Applying Equations A6.1b and A6.1c to Equation 30.19 yields $$ \nabla_{\mathbf{Z}}\big[w(\mathbf{z})\big]=\mathbf{b}+\gamma\mathbf{z} $$ (30.20a)

label: Formula 30.20b
section: MULTIDIMENSIONAL QUADRATIC FITNESS REGRESSIONS
subsection: Geometric Aspects
nearby_text: is the vector $ \mathbf{b} + \gamma \mathbf{z} $. If the true individual fitness surface is indeed a quadratic, the average gradient of individual fitness taken over the distribution of phenotypes is $$ \int\nabla_{\mathbf{Z}}[w(\mathbf{z})]p(\mathbf{z})\mathrm{d}\mathbf{z}=\mathbf{b}\int p(\mathbf{z})\mathrm{d}\mathbf{z}+\gamma\int\mathbf{z}p(\mathbf{z})\mathrm{d}\mathbf{z}=\mathbf{b} $$ (30.20b) as the last integral is $ \mu $ (which is zero by construction). Hence, if the true fitness function is quadratic, the average gradient of individual fitness is given by b, independent of the distrib

label: Formula 30.25
section: MULTIDIMENSIONAL QUADRATIC FITNESS REGRESSIONS
subsection: A Brief Digression: Orthonormal and Diagonalized Matrices
nearby_text: re scaled in the transformed system. Using the unitary property of U, premultiplying A by $ U^{T} $ and then postmultiplying by U results in a diagonal matrix whose elements are the eigenvalues of A, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{U}^{T}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{U}&=\mathbf{U}^{T}(\mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{U}^{T})\mathbf{U}=(\mathbf{U}^{T}\mathbf{U})\boldsymbol{\Lambda}(\mathbf{U}^{T}\mathbf{U})\\&=\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\end{aligned} $$ (30.25)

label: Formula 30.29
section: MULTIVARIATE SEMIPARAMETRIC FITNESS SURFACE ESTIMATION
subsection: Projection-pursuit Regression and Thin-plate Splines
nearby_text: iedman and Stuetzle 1981). The basic idea behind PPR is to approximate some complex function, $ f(\mathbf{z}) $, with a series of projection vectors, $ a_i $, and associated ridge functions, $ f_i $, $$ \begin{aligned}f(\mathbf{z})&\simeq f_{1}(\mathbf{a}_{1}^{T}\mathbf{z})+f_{2}(\mathbf{a}_{2}^{T}\mathbf{z})+\cdots+f_{k}(\mathbf{a}_{k}^{T}\mathbf{z})\\&=f_{1}(x_{1})+f_{x}(x_{2})+\cdots+f_{k}(x_{k})\end{aligned} $$ (30.29)

label: Formula 30.33b
section: PATH ANALYSIS AND FITNESS ESTIMATION
subsection: Morrissey's Extended Selection Gradient Vector, $ \eta $
nearby_text: $, even if they are causally connected though intermediates; and finally, we define $ B_{ii} = 0 $. With B defined in this fashion, Morrissey found that the values of $ \Phi_{ij} $ are calculated by $$ \boldsymbol{\Phi}=(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{B})^{-1} $$ (30.33b) From Equation 30.33a, we have $$ \boldsymbol{\eta}=\boldsymbol{\Phi}\boldsymbol{\beta}_{pa}=(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{B})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\beta}_{pa} $$ (30.33c)

label: Formula 30.35a
section: LEVELS OF SELECTION
subsection: Contextual Analysis
nearby_text: tive to using group means is to model neighbor interactions that influence fitness (e.g., Equation 22.59a). In such cases, the fitness of a focal individual, i, can be written (for a single trait) as $$ w_{i}=1+\beta_{1}z_{i}+\beta_{2}\Big(\sum_{i\neq j}z_{j}\Big)+e_{i} $$ (30.35a)

  "nearby_text": "tive to using group means is to model neighbor interactions that influence fitness (e.g., Equation 22.59a). In such cases, the fitness of a focal individual, i, can be written (for a single trait) as $$ w_{i}=1+\\beta_{1}z_{i}+\\beta_{2}\\Big(\\sum_{i\\neq j}z_{j}\\Big)+e_{i} $$ (30.35a)"
}

label: Formula 30.35b
section: LEVELS OF SELECTION
subsection: Contextual Analysis
nearby_text: d to the model using the standard selection on group model by noting that $$ \sum_{i\neq j}z_{j}=n\overline{z}-z_{i} $$ when i interacts with n - 1 neighbors. Hence, we can express Equation 30.35a as $$ \begin{aligned}w_{i}&=1+\beta_{1}z_{i}+\beta_{2}\Big(n\overline{z}-z_{i}\Big)+e_{i}\\&=1+\big(\beta_{1}-\beta_{2}\big)z_{i}+\beta_{2}n\overline{z}+e_{i}\end{aligned} $$ (30.35b)

  "nearby_text": ", and therefore only includes group means in the analysis. For a single trait, the investigator might consider the slope of a simple (i.e., univariate) regression of mean fitness on group trait mean, $$ \\overline{w}_{i}=1+\\beta\\overline{z}_{i}+e_{i} $$ (30.37a)"
}

label: Formula 30.37b
section: LEVELS OF SELECTION
subsection: Selection Differentials Can Be Misleading in Levels of Selection
nearby_text: If we recall (LW Chapter 3) that the slope of a univariate regression is the covariance divided by variance of the predictor variable, the slope becomes $$ \beta=\frac{\sigma(\overline{z}_{i},\overline{w}_{i})}{\sigma^{2}(\overline{z}_{i})} $$ (30.37b)

  "nearby_text": "If we recall (LW Chapter 3) that the slope of a univariate regression is the covariance divided by variance of the predictor variable, the slope becomes $$ \\beta=\\frac{\\sigma(\\overline{z}_{i},\\overline{w}_{i})}{\\sigma^{2}(\\overline{z}_{i})} $$ (30.37b)"
}

label: Formula 30.38a
section: LEVELS OF SELECTION
subsection: Hard, Soft, and Group Selection: A Contextual Analysis Viewpoint
nearby_text: , hard, soft, and strict group selection can all be modeled as special cases of a contextual analysis regression. For simplicity, we focus on a single trait, and the resulting regression has the form $$ w_{ij}=1+\beta_{1}z_{ij}+\beta_{2}\overline{z}_{i}+e_{ij} $$ (30.38a)

  "nearby_text": ", hard, soft, and strict group selection can all be modeled as special cases of a contextual analysis regression. For simplicity, we focus on a single trait, and the resulting regression has the form $$ w_{ij}=1+\\beta_{1}z_{ij}+\\beta_{2}\\overline{z}_{i}+e_{ij} $$ (30.38a)"
}

label: Formula 30.38b
section: LEVELS OF SELECTION
subsection: Hard, Soft, and Group Selection: A Contextual Analysis Viewpoint
nearby_text: We can also rewrite this regression in terms of selection on the within-group deviation plus selection on the group means, $$ w_{ij}=1+\beta_{1}^{*}\left(z_{ij}-\overline{z}_{i}\right)+\beta_{2}^{*}\overline{z}_{i}+e_{ij} $$ (30.38b)

        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}w(\\mathbf{z})&=a+\\mathbf{b}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{y}+\\frac{1}{2}\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{y}\\right)^{T}\\gamma(\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{y})=a+\\mathbf{b}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{y}+\\frac{1}{2}\\mathbf{y}^{T}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\gamma\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\mathbf{y}\\\\&=a+\\mathbf{b}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{y}+\\frac{1}{2}\\mathbf{y}^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{y}=a+\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\theta_{i}y_{i}+\\frac{1}{2}\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\lambda_{i}y_{i}^{2}\\end{aligned}",

  "nearby_text": "We can also rewrite this regression in terms of selection on the within-group deviation plus selection on the group means, $$ w_{ij}=1+\\beta_{1}^{*}\\left(z_{ij}-\\overline{z}_{i}\\right)+\\beta_{2}^{*}\\overline{z}_{i}+e_{ij} $$ (30.38b)"
}

label: Formula 30.38c
section: LEVELS OF SELECTION
subsection: Hard, Soft, and Group Selection: A Contextual Analysis Viewpoint
nearby_text: We use $ \beta_{i}^{*} $ to stress to the reader that the regression coefficients in Equation 30.38b can differ from those in Equation 30.38a. To see the connection, note that $$ w_{ij}=1+\beta_{1}^{*}z_{ij}+\left(\beta_{2}^{*}-\beta_{1}^{*}\right)\overline{z}_{i}+e_{ij} $$ (30.38c) showing that $ \beta_{1} $ is the same under both models $ (\beta_{1}=\beta_{1}^{*}) $, while $ \beta_{2}=\beta_{2}^{*}-\beta_{1}^{*} $.

        "section": "LEVELS OF SELECTION",

        "label": "Formula 30.38b",
        "section": "LEVELS OF SELECTION",
        "subsection": "Hard, Soft, and Group Selection: A Contextual Analysis Viewpoint",

  "nearby_text": "We use $ \\beta_{i}^{*} $ to stress to the reader that the regression coefficients in Equation 30.38b can differ from those in Equation 30.38a. To see the connection, note that $$ w_{ij}=1+\\beta_{1}^{*}z_{ij}+\\left(\\beta_{2}^{*}-\\beta_{1}^{*}\\right)\\overline{z}_{i}+e_{ij} $$ (30.38c) showing that $ \\beta_{1} $ is the same under both models $ (\\beta_{1}=\\beta_{1}^{*}) $, while $ \\beta_{2}=\\beta_{2}^{*}-\\beta_{1}^{*} $."
}

label: Formula 30.39a
section: LEVELS OF SELECTION
subsection: Hard, Soft, and Group Selection: A Contextual Analysis Viewpoint
nearby_text: with soft selection. Here selection is entirely based on within-group deviations (such as within-family selection; Chapter 21). Hence, $ \beta_{1} = -\beta_{2} $ as the regression of fitness becomes $$ \begin{aligned}w_{ij}&=1+\beta\left(z_{ij}-\overline{z}_{i}\right)+e_{ij}\\&=1+\beta z_{ij}-\beta\overline{z}_{i}+e_{ij}\end{aligned} $$ (30.39a)

label: Formula 30.41a
section: LEVELS OF SELECTION
subsection: Early Survival: Offspring or Maternal Fitness Component?
nearby_text: he mother's total fitness can be considered as the sum of two separate episodes: selection on early survival in her offspring, $ w_{o(t)} $, and selection on her maternal trait, $ w_{m(t-1)} $, where $$ w_{o(t)}=\alpha_{o(t)}+b_{m}z_{m(t-1)}+b_{o}z_{o(t)}+\epsilon_{(t)} $$ (30.41a) $$ w_{m(t-1)}=\alpha_{m(t-1)}+b_{d}z_{m(t-1)}+\epsilon_{(t-1)} $$ (30.41b) giving her total fitness as $$ w_{o(t)}+w_{m(t-1)}=\left(\alpha_{o(t)}+\alpha_{m(t-1)}\right)+b_{m}z_{m(t-1)}+b_{d}z_{m(t-1)

        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}S_{m}(\\mathrm{off})&=\\sigma(w_{(t)},z_{m(t)})\\\\&=\\sigma\\big(b_{m}z_{m(t-1)}+\\delta_{f}b_{d}z_{m(t)}+b_{o}z_{o(t)},z_{m(t)}\\big)\\\\&=b_{m}\\sigma(z_{m(t-1)},z_{m(t)})+\\delta_{f}b_{d}\\sigma(z_{m(t)},z_{m(t)})+b_{o}\\sigma(z_{o(t)},z_{m(t)})\\\\ \\end{aligned}",

  "nearby_text": "of the mother), and by $ b_{m}z_{m(t)} $ in Equation 30.40b (the cost to the offspring). Following the same logic leading to Equation 30.40d, the selection differential on the maternal trait becomes $$ \\begin{aligned}S_{m}(\\mathrm{mother})&=\\sigma(w_{o(t)}+w_{m(t-1)},z_{m(t-1)})\\\\&=\\frac{1}{2}\\Biggl[(b_{m}+b_{d})\\sigma^{2}(z_{m})+b_{o}\\sigma(z_{o(t)},z_{m(t-1)})\\Biggr]\\\\&=\\frac{1}{2}\\Biggl[(b_{m}+b_{d})\\sigma^{2}(z_{m})+b_{o}\\frac{\\sigma^{2}(A_{m},A_{o})}{2}\\Biggr]\\\\ \\end{aligned} $$ (30.41d)"
}

label: Formula 30.42

nearby_text: Subtracting Equation 30.41d from Equation 30.40d yields $$ S_{m}(\mathrm{mother})-S_{m}(\mathrm{off})=\frac{b_{m}}{2}\left[\sigma^{2}(z_{m})-\sigma^{2}(A_{m})\right]-\frac{3}{4}b_{o}\sigma(A_{m},A_{o}) $$ (30.42)

        "id": "formula_30.40c",
        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}S_{m}(\\mathrm{off})&=\\sigma(w_{(t)},z_{m(t)})\\\\&=\\sigma\\big(b_{m}z_{m(t-1)}+\\delta_{f}b_{d}z_{m(t)}+b_{o}z_{o(t)},z_{m(t)}\\big)\\\\&=b_{m}\\sigma(z_{m(t-1)},z_{m(t)})+\\delta_{f}b_{d}\\sigma(z_{m(t)},z_{m(t)})+b_{o}\\sigma(z_{o(t)},z_{m(t)})\\\\ \\end{aligned}",

      {
        "id": "formula_30.40d",
